problem_summary: 2011 MCM Problem B (discrete/network optimization): determine the minimal and adequate placement of VHF repeaters to serve a population of low-power radio users within a circular region of radius 40 miles, while minimizing the number of frequency bands used and keeping interference negligible. The standard hard version asks: 1000 users; the harder version asks 10000 users; and a stated extension considers mountainous (non-flat) terrain. This team modeled each repeater as a disk of radius 10 mi (18 ft antenna), greedily placed repeaters to cover the most users, assigned frequencies via graph coloring, and stress-tested against two synthetic population distributions plus complex terrain.
models_used: Disk/circle covering formulation: each repeater = a covering disk of radius r=10 mi (antenna height 18 ft); region = disk of radius 40 mi; coverage cast as the geometric Set/Disk Cover problem

Analytic area bound + hexagonal packing: lower bound n>=16 from area ratio (1600pi/100pi), upper bound n<=21 from a hexagonal lattice covering with radius-10 circles, giving the provable bracket 16<=n<=21

Greedy maximum-coverage algorithm: iteratively place a repeater at the grid point covering the most still-uncovered users; cited as a 2-approximation to the NP-hard maximum-coverage/set-cover decision problem

Utility-maximization generalization: each user's repeater redundancy modeled as a geometric series with downtime probability u (1st repeater utility 1, 2nd u, 3rd u^2, ...), summing to supremum 1/(1-u); basic coverage recovered as the special case u=0 (single codebase)

Graph vertex coloring for frequency assignment: build a conflict graph (repeaters = vertices; edge if dist(i,j) <= 2.5r, i.e. an extra half-radius interference buffer beyond overlap) and apply greedy graph coloring; each color = a distinct frequency
Barabasi-Albert preferential attachment model to synthesize realistic clustered (scale-free) population distributions, contrasted against a uniform-random distribution baseline

Radio-physics propagation models: tropospheric-bending radio horizon r~=sqrt(2h)~=1.41sqrt(h) (vs line-of-sight 1.23sqrt(h)); inverse-square free-space power density transitioning to inverse-fourth law from ground-plane reflection; Fresnel-zone diffraction discussion

Line-of-sight visibility over terrain: elevation-profile intersection test using bilinear interpolation (accuracy) or Bresenham's line algorithm (speed) to decide if two antennas have an unobstructed path

Procedural terrain generation: Earth-curvature base surface f(x,y)=sqrt(R^2-x^2-y^2)+sqrt(R^2-r^2)-R perturbed by the Diamond-Square fractal algorithm (or real elevation data) to create mountains/valleys
key_techniques: Sandwiching the answer with a provable bound (16<=n<=21) BEFORE running any heuristic — gives a verifiable yardstick to judge the greedy output against, and a guaranteed fallback when greedy underperforms

Unifying two distinct task goals (minimize repeaters for p% coverage; maximize coverage with n repeaters) into one monotone greedy sweep — recording cumulative coverage at each step yields both answers from a single run

Designing one parameterized algorithm (utility-based greedy with parameter u) so that the basic coverage model is literally the u=0 special case — minimizes code duplication and keeps the model family coherent

Explicit runtime/complexity analysis at each stage: O(s^4) quartic in grid fineness for basic greedy, O(s^4 log_u(1-W)) for utility version, reduced to O(r s^2 log(rs)) average-case via dynamic-programming precomputation of circle populations + a maintained sorted list; O(n^5) for full terrain coverage with a stated symmetry halving (A sees B => B sees A)

Treating relaxation of the 100% requirement as built-in sensitivity analysis: sweeping the coverage/utility target (80%/92%/100%) exposes the steep marginal cost of the last few percent (e.g. last 8% of users needs 13 extra repeaters for 1000 uniform users; 17 extra for 10000)
Monte Carlo averaging with uncertainty quantification: 100 runs per 1000-user curve and 20 runs per 10000-user curve, plotting mean +/- one standard deviation error bars (Figure 12)
Grounding arbitrary modeling choices in real data — UK band-plan with 127 operational repeaters using only 15 distinct frequencies validates the claim that a small palette (<=12 colors) suffices
Discretization of continuous space to a 1/4-mile grid so geometric covering becomes a tractable combinatorial search over grid points
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective this paper wins because it does three things rare in the same submission. (1) It pairs a rigorous theoretical bracket (provable 16<=n<=21 via area bound + hexagonal packing) with a practical heuristic (greedy 2-approximation), then closes the loop: when greedy exceeds 21 it falls back to the proven hexagonal covering, turning a heuristic weakness into a non-issue. (2) It demonstrates genuine CS/algorithmic maturity — correctly identifies the problem as NP-hard set/disk cover, cites the 2-approximation guarantee, derives explicit big-O runtimes, and engineers real optimizations (DP precomputation, sorted lists) rather than hand-waving. (3) It connects all three sub-questions (1000 users, 10000 users, mountains) through ONE coherent, parameterized model and validates against two population models (uniform + Barabasi-Albert preferential attachment), with Monte-Carlo averaging and error bars. The frequency-minimization requirement — which weaker teams ignore — is handled cleanly and correctly via graph coloring with a defensible interference buffer. The result is a model that is provably bounded, computationally honest, physically literate (radio horizon, Fresnel diffraction, inverse-fourth reflection), and empirically validated."
section_highlights: "Summary delivers concrete headline numbers (15 repeaters/1000 users, 20/10000; relaxing to 95% coverage drops these to 10 and 15; 21 always suffices) — a judge sees the answer in 30 seconds. Background (Sec 1) establishes real radio-engineering credibility (VHF 144-148 MHz band plan, 600 kHz repeater offset, PL codes, 15/20 kHz channel spacing) and the UK 127-repeater dataset that justifies a small frequency palette. Circle Covering (Sec 4) is the analytic spine: clean lower bound from area ratio, upper bound from hexagonal packing, with the honest note that finite-boundary disk covering is NP-complete and unsolved for n>=16. Our Approach (Sec 6) cleanly separates basic coverage, the utility generalization (with the geometric-series redundancy argument), and frequency selection, each with pseudocode (Programs 1-3) and runtime analysis. Results (Sec 7) systematically crosses {uniform, preferential} x {1000, 10000} x {coverage, utility} with the relaxation sweep, and Figure 12 adds Monte-Carlo error bars. Conclusions (Sec 8) is unusually candid: it openly states greedy is 'weak when 100% coverage is sought' but neutralizes the weakness with the hexagonal fallback. Future Considerations is genuinely substantive (ad-hoc/game-theoretic routing, dynamic users, Longley-Rice irregular terrain model) rather than filler."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Idealized circular coverage: a repeater is a perfect radius-10 disk on flat ground; the team admits this 'crudely approximates' real propagation and points to the Longley-Rice Irregular Terrain Model as the proper tool they did not fully integrate

The 18 ft / 10 mile core assumption is conceded to be 'for the most part arbitrary' — chosen to avoid the trivial single-center-repeater case rather than derived from the problem; the whole numeric answer scales with this choice

Greedy never beats the 21-repeater theoretical covering at 100% coverage and consistently needs more; the paper only rescues this by switching algorithms at the boundary rather than improving the heuristic itself
All data is synthetically generated (uniform + preferential attachment); no real city population distribution or real topographic map is used, which the team flags as the biggest realism gap

Interference/frequency model is simplified: it assumes once the required number of frequencies is small, assigning actual channel numbers (with the 600 kHz input/output shift) is 'trivial' and never produces a concrete channel plan; PL-code benefits are mentioned but not quantified

Terrain coverage computation is O(n^5) and the sequential DP optimizations are explicitly lost in the non-circular/terrain case, so the elevation-aware results are far less scalable than the flat-region results

Static, one-to-many communication only: users don't move, don't join/leave, and the model assumes an external backhaul (e.g. EchoLink) connecting repeaters rather than modeling repeater-to-repeater relay — all deferred to future work

Some figures show garbled/auto-extracted scatter data and a couple of typos (OCR-era artifacts), and the analytic NP-hardness citations carry the argument without an independent optimal-solution benchmark for mid-size n
reusable_patterns: Bound-then-heuristic sandwich: derive a cheap provable lower bound (area/counting) and a constructive upper bound (a lattice/packing you can exhibit), THEN run a heuristic inside that bracket and report the gap — works for any covering/packing/assignment problem

Heuristic-with-guaranteed-fallback: when your approximation can exceed a known optimal construction, detect that case and switch to the construction, so worst-case performance is bounded by a proof not by luck

One parameterized model, multiple deliverables: design the algorithm so each required sub-answer is a special case of a single parameter setting (here u=0 recovers basic coverage from the utility model) — reduces code, errors, and reviewer confusion

Relaxation-as-sensitivity-analysis: sweep the hard constraint (100% -> 95% -> 90% -> 80%) and plot the marginal cost curve; the 'last few percent is wildly expensive' insight is both a finding and a robustness check, and it directly answers 'minimal AND adequate'

Dual stress-test distributions: validate on an easy/idealized input (uniform) AND a realistic structured input (Barabasi-Albert preferential attachment / scale-free clustering); divergent behavior between them is itself a result

Monte Carlo with error bars as standard practice: average many runs, scale run-count to problem size, and always show +/- one standard deviation so randomized-heuristic results look like science, not a single lucky run

Map sub-problems to named canonical problems and cite their complexity (Disk Cover, Set Cover, Circle Packing, graph coloring, all NP-hard/complete) — signals literacy and justifies using approximation algorithms instead of claiming exact optimality

Reduce assignment/conflict constraints to graph coloring: whenever you must assign a scarce resource (frequencies, time slots, colors) such that 'too-close' entities must differ, build a conflict graph with a tunable adjacency threshold and color it
Always pair pseudocode with explicit big-O AND a concrete optimization (precompute + sorted structure / DP) — shows the model is not just describable but actually runnable at the required scale
Anchor arbitrary parameters and palette sizes to a real-world dataset (UK's 127 repeaters using 15 frequencies) to convert a guess into a justified assumption
